FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Years 2 and 4 (2018–2022), n=4,443 adolescents from 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multivariable linear and Poisson regression models examining associations between screen time and step count with cardiovascular risk factors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article, BMC Public Health 2026

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate found. Step 2: Composite exposure is "screen time" defined as hours spent "watching television and videos, playing video games, texting, social media, and video chat." Composite bundles TV, unspecified-platform video games, and non-phone items (television) without separate device-specific breakout. BINDING rule: multi-device aggregated with TV and unspecified gaming → NOT_MET.

notes: Screen time composite includes TV, which disqualifies under FT-IC2 Step 2. BINDING clarification: composites with non-phone items (TV, unspecified gaming) without per-device breakout fail IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Years 1–4, baseline N=11,878, ages 9–10 at baseline

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multi-level latent growth curve and multi-group models examining parent-child informant discrepancy by age of smartphone ownership

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article, Development and Psychopathology 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Smartphone ownership (age of first smartphone acquisition, phone-specific variable) paired with internalizing problems (individual-trait outcome: behavioral/mental health). Results tables (Table 3, Figures 1–2) report β coefficients for smartphone ownership predicting internalizing problems across developmental groups. E.g., Figure 1 shows β=.29*** for 8&under, β=.39*** for 13&14 group. This qualifies: phone-specific IV (age of smartphone ownership) × individual-trait DV (internalizing problems).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative estimates reported: regression β coefficients with standard errors and p-values for smartphone ownership → internalizing problems interaction effects. Table 3 provides parameter estimates (Est, SE, p) across all age groups and timepoints.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis (multi-level regression) with numeric results throughout (β, SE, p-values; significance testing reported in text and tables)

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone ownership appears as IV in longitudinal analysis (predicting internalizing problems). Cross-lagged/bidirectional structure or IV presence anywhere disqualifies FT-EC7.
notes: Strong phone-specific variable (age of smartphone ownership) with individual-trait outcome (internalizing problems). Multi-group analysis shows effects vary by age of acquisition and developmental stage.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, T1–T4 (baseline N=11,787, 3-year follow-up period)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: random-intercept crosslagged panel models examining bidirectional associations between electronic media use types and family conflict

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article, Journal of Family Psychology 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Paper reports separate type-specific EMU measures including "social electronic media" (texting + social media, SM-specific variable) paired with family conflict (individual-trait outcome: behavioral/relational). Results tables and figures (Table 4, Figures 3–4) report β coefficients for social EMU ↔ family conflict bidirectional paths. E.g., Figure 3 shows social EMU → family conflict path (β=.10**) and reverse (β=.01). This qualifies: SM-specific variable (social EMU) × individual-trait outcome (family conflict).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative estimates reported: regression β coefficients, standard errors, p-values for social EMU, video viewing EMU, and video game EMU predicting family conflict and vice versa (Table 4, Figures 1–5, Supplemental Table 4 with parameter estimates across all timepoints)

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with numeric results: cross-lagged panel model estimates (β, SE, p-values) reported for all EMU types × family conflict pathways

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal with cross-lagged/bidirectional structure. EMU appears as both IV (predicting family conflict) and DV (with family conflict as IV). Bidirectional design disqualifies FT-EC7.
notes: Paper disaggregates EMU into social (texting + social media), video viewing, and video game types. Social EMU qualifies as SM-specific; other types (video viewing, video games) are separate. Main results focus on bidirectional associations between total EMU and family conflict, with moderation by gender and media type differences reported.
